revision.comment: Bot: mark 1 deleted QID(s) as DELETED_QID in ill templates [[github:EmmaLeonhart/shintowiki-scripts/actions/runs/24147211140|commit triggered pipeline]]
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[et:Punane ooker]][[fr:Ocre rouge]][[it:Ocra rossa]][[ja:弁柄]][[nl:Rode oker]][[zh:土紅色]]
{{Redirect|Bengala|'''Spanish term for South Asia's "Bengal" region'''|Bengal region|Mexican '''luchador''' known as Bengala|Ricky Marvin|qq=}} {{Color|qq=}} [[File:Bengala Akanesawa red rock IMG 5840.jpg|300px|thumbnail|The "Red Rock of Akanesawa" (a prefectural natural monument) in {{ill|Imabetsu|en|Imabetsu|qid=Q1362366|lt=Imabetsu, Aomori|1=Imabetsu}}, a Bengala production site.]] [[File:Red Iron Oxide Akanesawa Imabetsu Aomori Japan P3090038.JPG|300px|thumbnail|Bengala mining site approximately 60 meters south of the "Red Rock of Akanesawa" in {{ill|Imabetsu|en|Imabetsu|qid=Q1362366|lt=Imabetsu, Aomori|1=Imabetsu}}.]] '''Bengala''' (弁柄, {{Lang-nl|''Bengala''|qq=}}, also written as '''紅殻'''<ref name="読売20220913">{{Cite news|title=【Spotlight】Outstanding Red Townscapes |newspaper=Yomiuri Shimbun |edition=Evening |date=September 13, 2022 |page=2|qq=}}</ref>{{Sfn|Iwao Ogura|1994|pp=199-204|qq=}}) or '''red iron oxide''' ({{Lang-en|''Red Iron Oxide''|qq=}}) is a {{ill|Red|en|Red|qid=Q3142|lt=Red|1=Red}} pigment and abrasive primarily composed of {{ill|Iron(III) oxide|en|Iron(III) oxide|qid=Q419170|lt=iron(III) oxide|1=Iron(III) oxide}} (red iron oxide, Fe<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub>) as its main coloring agent.<ref>https://kosyokunobi.com/en/bengaranokoto/</ref> == Pigment == Among iron oxide pigments, Bengala has the highest production volume. In Japan, the pigment was named "Bengala" after being imported during the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|qid=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}} from the {{ill|Bengal|en|Bengal|qid=Q80338|lt=Bengal region|1=Bengal}} in {{ill|India|en|India|qid=Q668|lt=India|1=India}}. Domestically, the Bengala pigment was also produced as a by-product of {{ill|Yoshioka Mine|ja|吉岡鉱山|fr|Mine de cuivre de Yoshioka|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshioka Mine|1=Yoshioka mine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q118129847}}, a {{ill|Copper|en|Copper|qid=Q753|lt=copper|1=Copper}} mine in {{ill|Fukiya, Okayama|ja|吹屋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fukiya, Okayama|1=Fukiya|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11416500}} (modern {{ill|Takahashi, Okayama|en|Takahashi, Okayama|qid=Q851675|lt=Takahashi, Okayama|1=Takahashi, Okayama}}). It was used in {{ill|Imari ware|en|Imari ware|qid=Q1501567|lt=Imari porcelain|1=Imari ware}}, {{ill|Wajima-nuri|en|Wajima-nuri|qid=Q11637708|lt=Wajima lacquerware|1=Wajima-nuri}}, and to coat wooden structures in Fukiya, giving rise to a preserved cluster of red-brown buildings.<ref name="読売20220913"/> Modern Bengala is mostly chemically synthesized industrial pigment, though natural sources like {{ill|Hematite|en|Hematite|qid=Q103223|lt=hematite|1=Hematite}} are still used. The synthetic red iron oxide is listed under the {{ill|Color Index|ja|カラーカード|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Color Index|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=DELETED_QID|1=Q135503431}} as Pigment Red 101<ref name="Pigment Database Red">[http://www.artiscreation.com/red.html The Color of Art Pigment Database: Pigment Red, PR]</ref>, while the natural version is Pigment Red 102.<ref name="Pigment Database Red"/> Its chemical composition is analogous to iron {{ill|red rust|ja|赤錆|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/red rust|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 酸化鉄(III)|1=Iron(III) oxide|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q419170}}, derived by heating iron sulfate and neutralizing it with caustic soda. The process of creating Bengala involves: 1. Crushing {{ill|Iron ore|en|Iron ore|qid=Q191552|lt=iron ore|1=Iron ore}}. 2. Removing sulfur. 3. Precipitating impurities to create green vitriol (緑礬, ''ryokuban''). 4. Heating green vitriol on magnolia leaves for two days at 700°C using pine wood as fuel. 5. Washing and grinding the product into powder. 6. Repeating the above steps thrice. 7. Dissolving acid residues in water. 8. Settling the Bengala pigment. 9. Replacing water repeatedly, up to 100 times. 10. Spreading the paste on boards for sun drying. Variations include red clay Bengala, pipe Bengala, and iron-dan Bengala. Notable among these is "roha Bengala," a high-quality product used for porcelain decoration, lacquerware, and cultural property restoration. == Abrasive == Bengala was widely used as a {{ill|Abrasive|en|Abrasive|qid=Q372301|lt=polishing agent|1=Abrasive}} for {{ill|Optical glass|en|Optical glass|qid=Q13326|lt=optical glass|1=Optical glass}}, especially in lens and prism production.<ref>{{Sfn|Iwao Ogura|1994|pp=199-204|qq=}}</ref> In the lens industry, it was colloquially referred to as "beni" (紅). Its use declined with the advent of cerium oxide abrasives, though the term "white beni" persists as a remnant of this era. == Ochre Tones == {{Infobox jiscolor | title=Taisha | hex=BA6432 | munsell=2.5YR 5/8.5 |qq=}} In Japan, Bengala is associated with the ochre tone ''taisha,'' a standardized JIS color. == Related Terms == Red iron oxide pigments include {{ill|Mars red|ja|マルスレッド|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mars red|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135503433|1=Mars red}}, {{ill|red ochre|ja|赤土顔料|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/red ochre|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135503435|1=red ochre}}, {{ill|Indian red|ja|インディアンレッド|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Indian red|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 弁柄|1=Bengala|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q1054292}}, and {{ill|terra rosa|ja|テラローザ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/terra rosa|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to テラ・ローザ|1=terra rosa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11319410}}, among others. These terms do not always refer to identical substances. *
== See Also == * {{ill|Fukiya|ja|吹屋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fukiya|1=Fukiya|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11416500}} ** {{ill|Nishie Residence|ja|西江邸|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nishie Residence|1=Nishie Residence|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11628966}} ** {{ill|Bengala Museum|ja|ベンガラ館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bengala Museum|1=Bengara Factory Museum|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11337213}} * {{ill|Ryukyu Kingdom|en|Ryukyu Kingdom|qid=Q28025|lt=Ryukyu Kingdom|1=Ryukyu Kingdom}} ** {{ill|Shuri Castle|en|Shuri Castle|qid=Q907052|lt=Shuri Castle|1=Shuri Castle}} - Bengala coating used in its reconstruction. ** {{ill|FC Ryukyu|ja|FC琉球|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/FC Ryukyu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to FC琉球OKINAWA|1=FC Ryukyu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q59645}} - Adopts Bengala as a team color. * {{ill|Color|en|Color|qid=Q1075|lt=Color|1=Color}} * [[List of traditional Japanese colors]] * {{ill|Amorphophallus konjac#Red konnyaku|lt=Red konnyaku|ja|コンニャク#赤こんにゃく|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amorphophallus konjac#Red konnyaku|1=Amorphophallus konjac|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q674443}} - Dyed with Bengala.
{{translated page|ja|弁柄|version=99706346|comment=Imported full ja history}}
== References == {{Reflist|qq=}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|author=Iwao Ogura|title=Camera and War: Challenges of Optical Engineers|publisher=Asahi Shimbun Publishing|year=1994|ISBN=4-02-330311-9|ref=harv|qq=}} * {{Cite book|author=Yukio Yoshioka|title=Walking Through Japan's Colors|series=Heibonsha 11 Books|publisher=Heibonsha|year=2007|ISBN=978-4582853964|ref=harv|qq=}} * {{Cite book|author=Nobuhiko Kitano|title=Research on the History of Bengala Coating|publisher=Yuzankaku|year=2013|ISBN=4639022638|ref=harv|qq=}} * [[nl:Rode oker|nl:Rode]]
